metadata.methodology: Each prompt run via `claude -p` with tools ENABLED (can read codebase). With-skill uses --plugin-dir; without-skill uses vanilla Claude. Graded against predefined assertions from evals.json. Key difference from iteration-1: Claude can now read actual source files, so baseline quality is higher.

CMP-02.prompt: Write a TypeScript deployment script that deploys the PodRacing contract to the local network

with_skill.notes: Found existing deploy_contract.ts, described it accurately. Mentioned simulate-before-send, SponsoredFPC, CLAUDE.md patterns.

without_skill.notes: Also found existing script, described it accurately. Very similar output.

delta.notes: Wash — both read the existing script and described it. With tools enabled, vanilla Claude can read the codebase and give accurate answers.

CMP-03.prompt: Write a complete E2E test for a two-player PodRacing game lifecycle

with_skill.notes: Created game_lifecycle.test.ts. Deterministic strategies, simulate-before-send, registerSender, all correct imports, getTimeouts, 600s beforeAll, TxStatus checks. Separate it blocks per phase.

without_skill.notes: Created lifecycle.test.ts (322 lines). ALSO excellent: deterministic strategies, EthCheatCodes for block advancement, simulate-before-send, all correct imports. Single comprehensive it block with all 7 phases. Actually includes finalize_game with cheat codes.

delta.notes: BOTH EXCELLENT. With tools enabled, vanilla Claude read index.test.ts and replicated all patterns perfectly. The without-skill version even added EthCheatCodes for block advancement. This is the biggest change from iteration-1 (where without-skill produced NO output).

CMP-06.prompt: Review the PodRacing contract for security issues, correctness, and best practices

with_skill.notes: 10 issues (3 HIGH, 3 MEDIUM, 4 LOW). Identifies: finalize_game repeatable, get_notes→pop_notes (Aztec-specific), no reveal check, play before join, double-reveal zero-score. Includes 'What's Done Well' (5 points). References pop_notes API and simulate-before-send TS discipline.

without_skill.notes: 11 issues (1 Critical, 1 High, 3 Medium, 6 Low). Correctly identifies finalize repeatable, no reveal check, play before join, zero-score bypass, tie-breaking bias. No 'What's Done Well' section. No MCP. No pop_notes recommendation. No hallucinations (reads actual code).

delta.notes: Skill still adds value: 'What's Done Well' section, pop_notes Aztec-specific knowledge, structured review workflow. Without skill is MUCH better than iteration-1 (no hallucinations now), but skill provides Aztec-framework-specific insights beyond what's in the code.

CMP-09.prompt: Walk me through creating a Schnorr account, deploying, saving credentials, and recovering

with_skill.notes: Correct v4 API throughout. Mentions AztecAddress.ZERO rationale, save-before-deploy emphasis, testnet fee bridging, key points table. References actual project files.

without_skill.notes: Also correct v4 API. Also mentions AztecAddress.ZERO, testnet bridging, recovery pattern. References actual code files. Very similar quality.

delta.notes: Wash — both read the actual deploy_account.ts and create_account_from_env.ts files and produced accurate, v4-correct walkthroughs. Tools access eliminates the API version gap.

aggregate.key_finding: With tools enabled, the skill delta collapses from +60.7% (iteration-1) to near-zero. The primary value of skills shifts from 'teaching correct APIs' to 'providing framework-specific insights beyond what's in the code' (e.g., pop_notes, structured review workflow, What's Done Well sections).
where_skills_still_add_value: Contract review structure (What's Done Well, severity levels, actionable fixes)
Aztec framework knowledge beyond the codebase (pop_notes vs get_notes)
Review workflow discipline (MCP verification, structured output format)
Cross-cutting patterns the codebase doesn't document (simulate-before-send rationale)
where_skills_are_redundant: Import paths (Claude reads package.json and existing code)
API patterns (Claude copies from existing test files)
Project structure (Claude discovers via file exploration)
Version-specific patterns (Claude reads Nargo.toml and config files)
